justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Breaking change to domain model clearly identified — called out as 'fundamental change to the core domain model' with HIGH risk rating. (2) New minimum-cardinality invariant stated in BR-1 ('A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times') with testable assertion. (3) Data migration impact for 2M existing records noted in BR-4, AC-8, and business_impact.risk. (4) External API breakage identified in BR-8, AC-5, edge cases, and business_impact with partner API versioning discussed. (5) 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as the first escalation — explicitly calls out that the spec does not enumerate what preferences are supported. (6) Architectural concerns flagged via escalations covering state machine design, recipient mutability, and privacy — suitable for MJ involvement. (7) confidence.level = 62, well within the <= 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough business rules — 8 rules with clear invariant/non-invariant distinction and justifications. BR-1 nails the minimum-cardinality invariant, BR-4 covers data migration integrity, BR-8 addresses partner API backward compatibility.
Rich edge cases beyond what was expected — same-address recipients, in-transit recipient addition, billing per-recipient, and reporting assumption breakage all surfaced.
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced with three concrete options and a pragmatic recommendation (start minimal, design extensible).
Confidence breakdown is well-structured — high/low confidence areas clearly separated, with 5 explicit assumptions documented.
Privacy implications raised as an escalation — a non-obvious concern that many analyses would miss.
Business impact analysis covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with 8 stakeholder groups identified and their specific impacts described.
Does not explicitly flag MJ by name for architectural migration planning, though the escalations clearly describe architectural decisions that would warrant MJ's involvement.

Existing invariant change ('A Shipment has exactly one Recipient' becoming false) is implicitly captured in the domain_analysis.business_context and the framing of the 1:1 to 1:N evolution, though not stated as a named broken invariant.

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified ('fundamental change to the core domain model'). Minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-7, business_impact, edge_cases). External API breakage identified (BR-8, AC-5, escalations). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced as primary escalation. MJ-level architectural concern flagged via escalations on migration strategy and completion semantics. Confidence level 52, well below the 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with 8 business rules, 8 acceptance criteria, 8 edge cases, and 4 structured escalations
Correctly identifies the breaking nature of the cardinality change and its cascading impact across data, APIs, operations, and pricing
BR-1 explicitly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with clear justification and testable assertion
BR-7 and BR-8 directly address data migration (2M records) and external API backward compatibility as invariants
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as first escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas — demonstrates appropriate epistemic humility given spec gaps
Surfaces additional ambiguities beyond what was expected: shipment completion semantics, recipient privacy, mid-transit modifications
Stakeholder impact analysis is comprehensive — identifies 7 affected groups including non-obvious ones like Finance/Billing and Customer Support
Proposes 'Primary Recipient' concept for backward compatibility — a practical domain modeling insight
Edge cases are operationally grounded: bulk recipients, mid-transit changes, partial delivery failures, conflicting preferences, webhook timing

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified as aggregate root redefinition (1:1→1:N). Minimum-cardinality invariant stated as BR-1. Data migration impact for 2M records addressed in BR-3 and business_impact. External API breakage identified in BR-6, edge cases, and escalations. Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced in multiple escalations. MJ escalation implicit via architectural escalations (partner API strategy, status model). Confidence level 42, well below 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — went well beyond minimum requirements with 7 business rules, 6 acceptance criteria, 7 edge cases, and 5 detailed escalations
Correctly identified the change as a fundamental redefinition of the aggregate root, not a simple additive change
BR-1 clearly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
BR-3 explicitly addresses backward compatibility and migration of 2M records with a testable assertion
External API breakage thoroughly analyzed with three transformation strategy options in escalations
Surfaced multiple ambiguities beyond just delivery preferences: shipment status model, billing, physical-vs-logical shipment, recipient mutability, primary recipient concept, privacy model
Confidence level of 42 is appropriately low given the extensive list of unresolved business questions
Edge cases are operationally grounded — partial cancellation, conflicting preferences, billing, and notifications all reflect real logistics domain concerns
Each escalation includes concrete options with trade-off analysis and a recommendation, making them actionable rather than just flags
No explicit mention of escalating to MJ by name for architectural migration plan — escalations are framed generically rather than routing to a specific team member
